ground_truth.title: AI-powered metadata workflows at the ICAEW Digital Archive
ground_truth.author: Craig McCarthy
ground_truth.main_content: Craig McCarthy is Digital Archive Manager at the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales (ICAEW). When we launched the ICAEW Digital Archive in early 2020, we did what many small teams do with a new system and limited staff: we prioritised preservation over description. We pushed thousands of digital objects into Preservica (our chosen digital preservation system) with only minimal metadata – and sometimes none at all. Our assumption was that most users would arrive via our traditional library catalogue, and would simply follow links through to the digital objects. A few years on, it was clear that this approach had reached its limits, and so we began exploring how we could use AI as an assistive tool in metadata creation – both for tackling our metadata backlog, and for future ingests. “Good enough” metadata wasn’t good enough. As our collections grew, our initial “minimal metadata” approach threw up a number of issues, including: Discovery friction: Expecting users to navigate the repository through a separate library catalogue created unnecessary barriers to discovery. Uncatalogued formats: We had ingested assets not covered by the library catalogue (such as audio-visual recordings and web archives) that had no descriptive records elsewhere. Insufficient detail: Our assets needed asset-level descriptions beyond what the library catalogue provided (which was often only collection-level metadata). In response, we tried to apply more detailed metadata manually, but unfortunately a lack of strict guidelines meant that different people described things in different ways and our practices changed over time. We also faced capacity constraints. Some assets – especially long webinars – would have required hours of watching, pausing, summarising and typing to describe properly. We just didn’t have that kind of time. The result was a two-fold discovery problem: many assets had minimal or missing metadata, and others had metadata that was inconsistently or incorrectly applied. In both cases, items were effectively undiscoverable. Clearly, the repository's metadata needed to be reviewed and corrected, but a completely manual audit was impractical due to the volume of assets. This pushed us to explore how AI – specifically large language models (LLMs) – could help. Engineering an effective prompt. We knew what needed to be built: a system that would provide individual assets (or their most informative portions) to an LLM, along with highly detailed instructions on how to extract metadata in a specific style, and return a structured response. As we set about developing such a system, our primary focus was on engineering an effective prompt – essentially a cataloguing manual turned into instructions for the model. Here, we began with simple experiments using OpenAI's GPT models, before progressively expanding the prompt with more fields and rules while testing on real assets. We used the OpenAI API to run these tests, and quickly discovered that the more specific the prompt, the better the results. Our final prompt is a detailed set of instructions that tells the model exactly what we want, including: The role it should adopt (a professional archivist). The specific metadata fields to extract (title, description, date, subjects, etc.). Formatting rules (e.g., dates in YYYY-MM-DD, subjects as a comma-separated list). Style guidelines (e.g., descriptions should be concise, neutral, and avoid promotional language). Examples of good and bad outputs. Rules for handling uncertainty (e.g., if a date is ambiguous, flag it for review). We also built in safeguards, such as requiring the model to explain its reasoning for certain decisions, which helps with transparency and quality control. Building the pipeline. With a robust prompt in hand, we needed a way to automate the process. Our digital preservation system, Preservica, has a REST API that allows us to extract asset information and metadata. We built a Python script that: Queries the Preservica API to get a list of assets that need metadata. For each asset, extracts the relevant content (e.g., the full text of a PDF, or a transcript of an audio file). Sends the content and our detailed prompt to the LLM via the OpenAI API. Parses the LLM's response and updates the asset's metadata in Preservica via the API. Logs all actions for auditing and troubleshooting. The script runs on a schedule, processing assets in batches. We started with a small pilot batch of 100 assets, manually reviewed the results, refined the prompt, and then scaled up. Results and lessons learned. So far, the system has processed thousands of assets, generating metadata that is consistently formatted and of surprisingly high quality. The time savings are enormous: what would have taken weeks of manual work now happens automatically overnight. However, the process isn't fully autonomous. Human review remains essential, especially for: Complex or ambiguous assets where the LLM might make plausible but incorrect guesses. Sensitive content where ethical judgment is required. Validating the LLM's suggested subject terms against our controlled vocabularies. We've also learned that AI-assisted metadata creation works best when you have clear guidelines to begin with. The process of engineering the prompt forced us to define exactly what “good” metadata looks like for our collections – something we hadn't fully articulated before. Looking ahead, we're exploring how to integrate this workflow into our ingest process for new assets, and how to use AI for other tasks like generating alt-text for images or identifying potential sensitivities in content. For any archive considering a similar approach, our advice is to start small, focus on prompt engineering, and always keep a human in the loop. AI won't replace archivists, but it can be a powerful tool for tackling the metadata backlog that so many of us face.
with: When we launched the ICAEW Digital Archive in early 2020, we did what many small teams do with a new system and limited staff: we prioritised preservation over description.

A few years on, it was clear that this approach had reached its limits, and so we began exploring how we could use AI as an assistive tool in metadata creation – both for tackling our metadata backlog, and for future ingests.

Our final prompt is a detailed set of instructions that tells the model exactly what we want, including: The role it should adopt (a professional archivist). The specific metadata fields to extract (title, description, date, subjects, etc.).

We built a Python script that: Queries the Preservica API to get a list of assets that need metadata. For each asset, extracts the relevant content (e.g., the full text of a PDF, or a transcript of an audio file). Sends the content and our detailed prompt to the LLM via the OpenAI API.

So far, the system has processed thousands of assets, generating metadata that is consistently formatted and of surprisingly high quality. The time savings are enormous: what would have taken weeks of manual work now happens automatically overnight.
